explanation: The statement says that 'prop_0 must be preceded by prop_1', which means that whenever prop_0 occurs, prop_1 must have happened sometime in the past (possibly immediately before). In LTL, this is typically written so that 'it's never the case that prop_0 occurs before prop_1 has occurred', i.e., globally, if prop_0 happens, then previously (or at the same moment) prop_1 has occurred. The standard way in LTL for 'p must be preceded by q' is G(prop_0 -> (not prop_0) U prop_1), but more properly it is G(prop_0 -> F^- (prop_1)), but since we do not have past operators in basic LTL, a common workaround is: globally, if prop_0 occurs, prop_1 must have occurred sometime earlier (which is not directly expressible, but if prop_1 must directly precede prop_0, then X(prop_0) -> prop_1). Absent past operators, the strongest we can do is to say that every occurrence of prop_0 is preceded by an occurrence of prop_1. That is, it is never the case that we have a sequence where prop_0 becomes true in the absence of prop_1 having been true before. So, we use: !(true U (prop_0 & !(true U prop_1))) or G(prop_0 -> (not prop_0) U prop_1).

The requirement is that prop_0 must always be preceded by prop_1, analogous to saying that anytime prop_0 occurs, it was preceded at some earlier point (possibly immediately) by prop_1. This is often formalized as: it is never the case that prop_0 occurs and in the past, prop_1 did not occur. In LTL (which is future-looking), this is usually phrased such that whenever prop_0 occurs, then previously there was a prop_1, but in typical LTL only future direction formulas are expressible. However, for standard LTL, the property is stated as: "it is never the case that prop_0 happens before prop_1." That is, until a prop_1 occurs, prop_0 cannot occur. More precisely, (!prop_0) U prop_1: prop_0 does not happen until prop_1 happens. Then, this must hold globally, i.e., at every point, either prop_0 hasn't happened before prop_1, or for any occurrence of prop_0, prop_1 must have occurred before.

The specification states that whenever prop_0 occurs, it must have been immediately preceded by prop_1. In LTL, this is expressed by stating that globally, if prop_0 holds, then in the previous step (which can be tricky in standard LTL, but can be written as: it is never the case that prop_0 holds and prop_1 did not hold in the previous step). This can also be restated using the X (next) operator: globally, it is not the case that in the next step prop_0 holds and prop_1 does not hold now. For every DM_i simply means it applies universally to all instances; in LTL we'd specify it parametrically, but if just given as prop_0/prop_1, the formula is: G(!(X(prop_0) & !prop_1)), which is equivalent to G(prop_1 -> X(prop_0) -> false), i.e., at every moment if prop_0 holds in the next state, prop_1 must hold now.

The statement requires that every occurrence of 'prop_0' is always immediately after (i.e., preceded by) a 'prop_1', for all time. In LTL, this is commonly specified by ensuring that if 'prop_0' holds now, then in the previous state, 'prop_1' held. However, classical LTL does not include a 'previous' operator, so we must express this property by forbidding any occurrence of 'prop_0' that is not immediately after 'prop_1'. This can be encoded by stating that it is never the case that 'prop_0' holds and 'prop_1' did not hold in the previous time step. A standard way to do this in future-only LTL is to require that between any two occurrences of 'prop_0', a 'prop_1' must occur, and at the very start, 'prop_0' cannot occur. Alternatively, we can express: It is always the case that if 'prop_0' holds now, then in the previous moment 'prop_1' held, but as we lack 'previous', this is encoded as 'it is never the case that prop_0 occurs without being immediately preceded by prop_1'. This is captured with: G (prop_0 -> X (!prop_0 U prop_1)), which reads: Whenever 'prop_0' occurs, it must have been preceded (i.e., since the previous 'prop_0', a 'prop_1' must have occurred), so no repeated 'prop_0's without an intervening 'prop_1'.

The requirement is that every time prop_0 occurs, it must have been preceded by prop_1. This is typically captured by stating that it is never the case that prop_0 happens without a prior prop_1. An equivalent LTL formula expresses that whenever prop_0 occurs, prop_1 has occurred somewhere in the past (which in standard LTL is more conveniently written: 'It is always the case that if prop_0 occurs, then previously prop_1 occurred'). However, pure LTL does not have past operators, so we can use an equivalent formulation: 'It is always the case that if prop_0 occurs, then before that, prop_1 occurred and there was no other prop_0 before that.'

A classic LTL encoding is: G (prop_0 -> (¬prop_0 U prop_1)).

The statement says that 'a prop_0 must be preceded by a prop_1', which in LTL means that whenever prop_0 occurs, there was a prop_1 at some point before (in the past). Since LTL is future-looking, we can equivalently say: 'It is always the case that if prop_0 happens, then previously prop_1 must have been true.' In standard LTL, this is rephrased as: it is always true that prop_0 cannot occur unless prop_1 has already happened. This is captured by 'not prop_0 until prop_1', so the canonical LTL for this requirement is G ( prop_0 -> ( (!prop_0) U prop_1 ) ).

The property requires that whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must have occurred immediately before. In LTL, this is captured as 'if prop_0 holds, then in the previous step prop_1 was true.' Since standard LTL does not have a 'previous' operator, we rephrase: 'It is never the case that prop_0 holds at time 0, and thereafter, prop_0 can only occur directly after prop_1.' In LTL syntax, this is typically written as 'globally, if prop_0 holds, then previously (in the immediate previous time point), prop_1 held.' We encode this in LTL as: globally, if prop_0 holds, then in the previous time step, prop_1 held. Since standard LTL does not have 'previous', the property can also be phrased as: it is never the case that prop_0 holds and in the previous step prop_1 did not hold. Or, as a safety property: globally, (if the next state is prop_0, then currently prop_1 holds). This is encoded as: G (X prop_0 -> prop_1).

The property states that every occurrence of prop_0 must be preceded by a prop_1. In LTL, this is typically modeled as: it is always the case that if prop_0 occurs, then it must have been preceded by prop_1. Since LTL has no direct 'past' operators, the standard pattern to enforce this is: globally, it is not the case that prop_0 occurs without prop_1 having already occurred in the past, or that the first occurrence of prop_0 must be at least after (never before) a prop_1. This is encoded by stating that it is globally not the case that prop_0 occurs and prop_1 has not yet happened. In LTL, this can be written as: globally, if prop_0 occurs, then previously prop_1 must have happened, which, without past operators, is equivalent to: prop_0 cannot occur before prop_1 occurs. Thus, globally, if not prop_1 has occurred yet, then prop_0 does not occur.

The requirement states that every occurrence of prop_0 must be preceded by prop_1. In LTL, this is typically captured by saying that if prop_0 occurs, then prop_1 had to have occurred previously (possibly in the same state, if allowed). In a strict sense (no simultaneous occurrence), we specify that at any point when prop_0 occurs, prop_1 must have occurred before. This is expressed as globally: if prop_0 is now, then before now, prop_1 occurred. Using the weak until (release) operator in LTL: '!prop_0 V prop_1' expresses that prop_1 must be true up to and including the first occurrence of prop_0. But to enforce 'must be preceded', we use globally: not (prop_0 & not previously prop_1). In standard LTL, this is commonly encoded as G(prop_0 -> F in past prop_1), but as we cannot use past operators, the standard alternative is G( (!prop_1) U prop_0 -> false), which is equivalent to G( prop_0 -> (!prop_0 U prop_1) ). Hence, we say: at every occurrence of prop_0, before it, prop_1 must have occurred, i.e., it cannot be the case that prop_0 occurs without prop_1 ever occurring earlier. This is universally known in the LTL literature as G( prop_0 -> ( (!prop_0) U prop_1 ) ), but as we cannot reference the past directly, an equivalent is G( prop_0 -> (F prop_1) ), but that states prop_1 will occur after, not before. The best standard idiom is: globally, prop_0 can only occur after prop_1, meaning, until prop_1 occurs, prop_0 cannot occur: G( (!prop_1) -> (!prop_0 U prop_1) ), or globally, as long as prop_1 has not occurred, prop_0 cannot occur. This is cleanly captured as G( (!prop_1) -> (!prop_0) ). But the correct LTL idiom is G( (¬prop_1) U prop_0 -> false ), or G( prop_0 -> (prop_1 | (X (¬prop_1 U prop_1))) ). The simplest correct form is: it is never the case that prop_0 occurs before prop_1: G( (!prop_1) U prop_0 -> false ). Equivalently, at any time, if prop_0 occurs, then prop_1 occurred at or before that point: G( prop_0 -> ( (prop_1) | (previously) ) ). With standard LTL, the best you can write is: 'prop_1' must occur before 'prop_0', so 'prop_0' cannot occur before 'prop_1': (!prop_1) U prop_0 -> false. We can phrase this as 'it is always the case that if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must have occurred at some previous point.' So, 'prop_0 -> F[0:1] prop_1', but that is not expressible in basic LTL without past. Thus, the canonical encoding is: (!prop_1) U prop_0 cannot happen, so G( !((!prop_1) U prop_0)).

The requirement is that every occurrence of prop_0 is preceded by a prop_1. In LTL, this can be formulated as: whenever prop_0 occurs, in the previous time step, prop_1 was true. Since standard LTL does not have a direct 'previous' operator, we express this property equivalently as: it is always the case that, if prop_0 is true, then previously prop_1 was true. This can also be formulated by stating that it is never the case that prop_0 occurs without a prop_1 occurring earlier without encountering another prop_0 in between.
